title: Enhanced quantum metrology by criticality-assisted noncommutative preparation
authors: Ningxin Kong, Matteo G. A. Paris, Qiongyi He
abstract: Quantum criticality is a resource for quantum-enhanced metrology, but existing schemes face intrinsic limitations. These arise because using criticality directly in the encoding dynamics restricts the accessible parameters to those explicitly supported by the critical Hamiltonian, and the requirement for critical conditions narrows the effective estimation range. To solve this, we introduce a general framework termed criticality-assisted noncommutative preparation (CANP). In this approach, critical evolution is employed as a state-preparation resource. We establish the underlying algebraic conditions and show that the intrinsic noncommutativity between the preparation and encoding operations leads to a genuine enhancement of the quantum Fisher information (QFI). Remarkably, this enhancement may be achieved at fixed total sensing time and energy cost. The effect is quantified by the Wigner-Yanase skew information, which measures noncommutativity and exhibits the same critical scaling as the QFI. We demonstrate effective use of CANP in the quantum Rabi and Lipkin-Meshkov-Glick models. Our results establish CANP as a robust technique to effectively implement criticality-enhanced quantum metrology.
summary: This paper introduces a new protocol called CANP that uses quantum criticality to prepare states for enhanced parameter estimation. By separating the critical evolution from the parameter encoding, the method overcomes the limitations of previous schemes and allows for more versatile and efficient quantum sensing.
main_problem: Existing quantum metrology schemes using criticality are limited because they are restricted to parameters present in the critical Hamiltonian and suffer from a narrow estimation range.
main_result: The authors propose a 'criticality-assisted noncommutative preparation' (CANP) protocol that uses critical evolution solely for state preparation, enabling enhanced sensitivity to a broader range of parameters and achieving higher precision at fixed energy and time costs.
method: A two-stage protocol involving a preparation stage under a critical Hamiltonian followed by an encoding stage under a separate Hamiltonian, analyzed using the Quantum Fisher Information and Wigner-Yanase skew information.
model_or_system: The framework is demonstrated using the Quantum Rabi model (describing a spin interacting with a bosonic mode) and the Lipkin-Meshkov-Glick (LMG) model.
key_observables: Quantum Fisher Information (QFI) and Wigner-Yanase skew information.
important_parameters: Critical parameter delta (proximity to criticality), total sensing time T, and the enhancement ratio R(theta).
main_assumptions: The LMG model analysis assumes a low-excitation regime; the protocol's effectiveness relies on a specific algebraic noncommutativity relation.
figures_summary: Figure 1 provides a schematic comparison of the proposed protocol versus existing methods.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the CANP framework, establishes the algebraic conditions for enhancement, demonstrates the protocol using the Quantum Rabi and LMG models, and quantifies the metrological gain and scaling regimes.
why_interesting: It provides a new way to utilize phase transitions as a resource for sensing without being tied to the specific parameters of the phase transition itself, which is highly relevant for quantum metrology and many-body dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic diagram of the quantum metrological protocols. (a) In the direct-encoding scheme, a single probe state ρ0 is directly subjected to the parameter-encoding operation ˆUθ for a total encoding time T. (b) In our CANP protocol, the probe ρ is first prepared by the critical operation ˆUc and subsequently acquires a dependence on the parameter θ through ˆUθ. The preparation and encoding durations are denoted by tc and tθ, respectively, with the total evolution time satisfying T = tc + tθ.
FIG. 2. (a) QFI enhancement ratio R(θ) as functions of the critical-evolution time √
FIG. 3. (a) Wigner–Yanase skew information S(θ) and the QFI F(θ) as functions of the critical-evolution duration √
